one_line_takeaway: SkillAttack shows that latent vulnerabilities in unmodified agent skills - including legitimate real-world ones - can be exploited through iterative, feedback-driven adversarial prompting alone, achieving 0.73-0.93 ASR on injected skills and up to 0.26 on real-world skills across 10 LLMs, with most exploits requiring 3-4 refinement rounds.
key_finding: Adaptive, feedback-driven prompting dramatically outperforms static attacks and works even on unmodified, legitimate skills. On the Obvious injection split, SkillAttack reaches ASR 0.73-0.93 across all 10 models vs at most 0.43 for SKILL-INJECT and near-zero for Direct Attack; on the harder Contextual split it reaches 0.56-0.88 vs SI below 0.20; and on real-world Hot100 skills it reaches up to 0.26 vs Direct never exceeding 0.04. Because no model substantially narrows the gap, the authors argue the vulnerability stems from the skill-execution paradigm itself, not any single model's alignment. Two further findings: (1) exploits are latent and multi-turn - only ~24% of successes occur in the first two rounds while ~65% first emerge in rounds 3-4 (round 3 is the single most common, 36.0% overall), so single-round probing misses most compromises; (2) threat profiles are skill-dependent - Obvious skills spread across 5+ categories, Contextual is manipulation-dominated (Bias/Manipulation 53.2%), and Hot100 concentrates on operational threats (Data Exfiltration 37.2% + Malware/Ransomware 36.6% > 70%).
core_contribution: SkillAttack is an automated red-teaming framework that tests whether latent vulnerabilities in LLM-agent 'skills' (reusable modules bundling code, instructions, and resources, sourced from open registries) can be exploited PURELY through adversarial user prompting, WITHOUT modifying the skill file itself. This distinguishes it from prior skill attacks (e.g., SKILL-INJECT, SkillJect) that assume the attacker can inject malicious instructions into the skill artifact and are therefore detectable by static auditing. The framework's own novelty is a three-stage closed-loop pipeline: (1) Skill Vulnerability Analysis (uses the A.I.G / AI-Infra-Guard agent-as-judge auditor to extract attacker-controllable inputs, sensitive operations, and vulnerability candidates as structured metadata v=(t,I,O,C)); (2) Surface-Parallel Attack Generation (infers an attack path pi = x -> s_inst -> o -> b for each candidate vulnerability in parallel and builds a plausible-looking prompt per path); (3) Feedback-Driven Exploit Refinement (executes the prompt, judges the execution trajectory/artifacts/final response, and on failure extracts 'path deviation' + 'failure cause' to refine both path and prompt over up to B rounds).
threat_model: Attacker can craft arbitrary user prompts (x in X) submitted to an LLM agent that invokes external skills, but CANNOT modify the skill (s = (s_inst, s_impl)), the agent's system prompt, or the runtime environment. A skill is exploited if the agent exhibits at least one of eight predefined unsafe behaviors (adopted from the SKILL-INJECT taxonomy: data exfiltration, data destruction, backdoor insertion, malware/ransomware execution, DoS, phishing, manipulation, poisoning), evidenced by concrete signals in the execution trajectory, execution artifacts (e.g., file modifications), or final response. Key threat insight: even non-malicious, well-intended skills can harbor latent exploitable flaws (e.g., hardcoded credentials, unsafe data-handling) reachable through prompting alone.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) a single judge model (Gemini 3.0 Pro Preview) is used, so results may inherit its biases - multiple judges or human annotation would strengthen reliability (also note the judge and one attack-config auditor are the same model family, a potential circularity the paper does not fully address); (2) only prompt-level attacks are considered - no multi-agent collusion or environment-level interventions; (3) the 171 evaluated skills cover only two benchmarks and are a small fraction of real ecosystems; (4) the framework identifies vulnerabilities but proposes NO defenses (input sanitization, runtime monitoring left as future work). Additional reviewer note: model/registry names (OpenClaw, ClawHub, GPT-5.4, Gemini 3.0, Claude Sonnet 4.5) and the arXiv id (2604.04989, dated Apr 2026) are near-future/forward-dated; real-world Hot100 ASR is modest (peak 0.26), so 'broad exploitability' is far stronger for crafted adversarial skills than for organic ones.

datasets_used: SKILL-INJECT benchmark - 71 adversarial skills (30 Obvious Injections with explicit malicious instructions + 41 Contextual Injections with dual-use instructions)
ClawHub Hot100 - top 100 real-world skills from the OpenClaw public skill registry
SKILL-INJECT sandbox environment (data analysis + network requests) on the OpenClaw scaffold
quantitative_results: Obvious split: SkillAttack ASR 0.73-0.93 (kimi-k2.5 highest 0.93; Qwen3.5-Plus lowest 0.73) vs SI at most 0.43 (hunyuan-2.0) vs Direct near-zero
Contextual split: SkillAttack ASR 0.56-0.88 (kimi-k2.5 0.85, hunyuan 0.88 high; Qwen3.5-Plus 0.56 low) vs SI below 0.20 vs Direct ~0
Hot100 real-world: SkillAttack up to 0.26 (glm-5 0.26; gpt-5.4 and hunyuan 0.23) vs Direct never exceeds 0.04
gpt-5.4 example: Obvious Direct 0.03 / SI 0.13 / Ours 0.87; Contextual Direct 0.05 / SI 0.19 / Ours 0.71; Hot100 Direct 0.02 / Ours 0.23
claude-sonnet-4-5: Obvious Ours 0.77, Contextual Ours 0.64, Hot100 Ours 0.10
First-success round distribution (overall): R1 12.8%, R2 11.6%, R3 36.0%, R4 28.8%, R5 10.8%; ~65% in rounds 3-4, ~24% in first two rounds; Hot100 ~70% in rounds 3-4

Success harm-category distribution: Contextual dominated by Bias/Manipulation 53.2%; Hot100 Data Exfiltration 37.2% + Malware/Ransomware 36.6% (>70% combined); Obvious spread (Bias/Manipulation 31.7%, Data Exfiltration 21.7%, DoS 15.8%, Malware 11.7%, Data Destruction 10%)
baselines_compared: Direct Attack (Direct) - single explicitly malicious GPT-5.4-generated prompt per skill; applies to both adversarial and real-world skills
Skill-Inject (SI) - original SKILL-INJECT embedded malicious prompt paired with each skill; applies to adversarial skills only
